ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700); no specific Hyderabadi chicken dum biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW represents biryani as a rice-based meat dish; no Hyderabadi-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for biryani or Hyderabadi chicken dum biryani. SNOMED CT does not cover prepared ethnic rice dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Hyderabadi Chicken Dum Biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of fragrant basmati rice layered with spiced boneless chicken, fried onions, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), composite dish entry; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for basmati rice (ID 169704) and cooked chicken (ID 171477) for micronutrient cross-referencing.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports chicken biryani as a composite dish (per 100 g edible portion). Hyderabadi dum biryani is a regional variant with higher fat content due to ghee and fried onions; values are adapted upward for fat and energy based on standard Hyderabadi recipe proportions documented in ICMR-NIN recipe analysis methodology. Micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for basmati rice and cooked chicken breast components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice-based mixed dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hyderabadi Chicken Dum Biryani. GI estimated at 50 based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice (GI ~50–58, mean ~52); (2) the dum cooking method with whole spices, fat (ghee), protein (chicken), and fibre components that collectively lower the glycemic response relative to plain rice; (3) Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) reporting GI of 50 for a chicken and rice mixed dish. GI of 50 assigned; gi_category = low (≤55). Glycemic load per 400 g serving: (21.5 g carb per 100 g × 4) × 50 / 100 = 43.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1520 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving (approximately 200 g) to keep sodium intake below 760 mg per meal, and request reduced salt and no added MSG when ordering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~50) and high carbohydrate load per restaurant serving (~86 g per 400 g plate)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion to approximately 150–200 g to limit glycemic load, pair with a non-starchy vegetable side, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the substantial carbohydrate content of basmati rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and fried onions (~2.8 g per 100 g; ~11.2 g per serving) and moderate cholesterol (~42 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and request preparation with minimal ghee, as the saturated fat content from ghee and deep-fried onions contributes meaningfully to daily saturated fat limits recommended by cardiovascular guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Chicken Dum Biryani is a celebrated South Asian rice dish originating from Hyderabad, India, and is one of the most iconic preparations in Mughal-influenced Indian cuisine. It is prepared using the 'dum' technique, in which marinated boneless chicken pieces and parboiled basmati rice are layered in a heavy-bottomed pot or handi, sealed with dough or a tight-fitting lid, and slow-cooked over a low flame so that the steam circulates internally, allowing the rice to absorb the aromatic spiced chicken gravy. The marinade typically includes yoghurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf), and saffron-infused milk. Characteristic additions include golden fried onions (birista), fresh mint leaves, and almonds, which contribute richness, fragrance, and textural contrast. The resulting dish has a deeply aromatic, slightly smoky character with tender, juicy chicken and long-grain rice grains that are separate yet flavour-saturated. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 185 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat (including 2.8 g saturated fat from ghee), and 21.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low GI), though a full restaurant serving of 400 g delivers a substantial glycemic load of approximately 43 g. Sodium content is notable at ~380 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is halal and gluten-free but contains dairy (ghee, yoghurt) and tree nuts (almonds). Clinically, it is a moderate-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes due to high carbohydrate load per serving, and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from ghee. It is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is a popular celebratory and weekend meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Hyderabadi Chicken Dum Biryani?
Is chicken biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of biryani?
How much protein in chicken biryani?
Hyderabadi biryani vs Lucknowi biryani difference
Can I eat biryani with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani composite dish data (closest approved primary source for Indian dishes) with upward fat adjustment for Hyderabadi dum style preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice entries and Atkinson et al. 2008 mixed rice-chicken dish data. No exact Hyderabadi Chicken Dum Biryani entry exists in any approved primary source; hence confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy for this dish type. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for chicken biryani composite entries. 3. Nutrient values represent a typical Hyderabadi dum biryani preparation; restaurant-specific values (Aryana, South Bridge Road) may vary depending on ghee quantity, chicken-to-rice ratio, and portion size. 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for this specific dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 5. The input food_name 'Hyderbadi' is a common misspelling; canonical spelling 'Hyderabadi' is used in this record. 6. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard Indian restaurant plate; actual restaurant portion may vary. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (North Indian restaurant, Singapore); formal halal certification status of the specific restaurant was not verified from an approved source.
